title: Support seccomp-notify-socket and seccomp-notify-plugins option sent to conmon
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind feature

**Description**
Latest conmon support two new options: `--seccomp-notify-socket` and `--seccomp-notify-plugins` which allows user to load a dynamic library as a plugin to handle seccomp user notification. However, there is no way to pass those two options to conmon. [Here](https://www.scrivano.org/posts/2020-08-10-seccomp-notifications/) is a good blog post describing what those two options are used for. More generally, I see that `podman run` support general `--runtime-arg` for arguments being sent to runc. Wondering if it make sense to have something like `--conmon-arg` to so that I can pass those two options to conmon.

comments.body:
Thanks for reaching out, @sfc-gh-hyu!

@giuseppe PTAL

I've tried to do that in the past but I've not come up with a nice UX.  Do you have any suggestions how this should look like without the user having to know about internal details as seccomp-notify-socket)?

@giuseppe Yeah I don't have a good answer either. It feels like as an enduser, I don't care too much about where the `seccomp-notify-socket` is located. It would be best that podman can handle/manage this unix socket. However, I do need to pass in the path to the dynamic library (a.k.a seccomp-notify-plugins), which will be loaded by conmon.
@giuseppe Can we just add a new option `--conmon-seccomp-notify-plugin`?

sure.

Would you like to open a PR?  That would be the quickest way to move forward
Sure. Do you have any recommendation/best practice on where to put this `seccomp-notify-socket`?

do you mean `--conmon-seccomp-notify-plugin`?

To start playing with it, I'd suggest it becomes part of `--security-opt`, e.g. `--security-opt seccomp-notify-plugins=....`

No what I mean is that ideally, we want to hide `seccomp-notify-socket` from end users. End users only need to pass the path to the plugin ( a.k.a. a dynamic library). But conmon need a unix domain socket so that kernel can return the notification back to userspace. I think podman should mange this unix domain socket, i.e. create this unix domain socket somewhere in the filesystem, and pass this unix domain socket to conmon. I am asking do you have an idea on where we should create a such unix domain socket file?
it should probably be some internal path under the podman rundir (e.g. `/run/user/1000/libpod/tmp/$CONTAINER_ID-notify`)
Sounds good. I will see if I can figure something out and submit a pr.
How would Podman know to do this?

@rhatdan I am not sure if I understand question correctly. I am thinking that if `--conmon-seccomp-notify-plugins` is provided to podman during the run or exec command, then podman should try to setup socket, and pass to both plugins and socket to conmon?

Ok so you are saying the user would do somethin like:

podman run --security-opt seccomp:notify ...

I would imagine that for certain syscalls, user will say ALLOW or KILL directly, but for other syscalls, it will deferred the decision to the user space. Check example here: https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man2/seccomp_unotify.2.html It really just depends on how user write the seccomp bpf program, ( or how the seccomp policy file is written ). So I think user would do something like `podman run --security-opt seccomp=profile.json --conmon-seccomp-notify-plugins /usr/local/lib64/syscall_handle.so ...`, while json will say for syscall like `clone`, it will do a `SECCOMP_RET_USER_NOTIF`. And if podman see that `.so` file is specified, it will create a unix domain socket to receive notification from kernel, and pass that uds to conmon.
Shouldn't Podman just review the profile.json file looking for SECCOMP_RET_USER_NOTIF and setup the socket?  User would not need to add input?
Yeah, I think that should work as well. I am not aware that podman has the capability to parse the content of profile.json.
It doesn't but it could be added, WDYT @giuseppe
yeah I would be open to such behavior
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@giuseppe @haircommander Did anything ever happen with this?

not that I am aware of.

@sfc-gh-hyu are you still interested?
I don't have bandwidth to do it now. You can close it for now. If I need it, I will re-open the issue.
